summary: Gruber argues that with the iPhone 3G, Apple is aggressively pursuing smartphone market share by halving the price to $199 rather than adding premium hardware features. The new model adopts the traditional carrier subsidy model, trading Apple's cut of monthly fees for a much lower entry price. Apple is expanding to 70 countries and Gruber predicts 20 million units sold in 2008. He frames this as a deliberate two-step strategy: first grab market share with cheap hardware, then lock users in through the App Store platform and enterprise apps.
key_points: Apple chose to halve the iPhone's price rather than load it with new hardware features, signaling a market share play
The shift to carrier subsidies means in-store activation and losing monthly revenue share, but enables the $199 price point
Gruber predicts 20 million iPhones sold in 2008, with 10 million in the holiday quarter alone
The real strategy is a two-step: grab market share with cheap hardware, then entrench users with the App Store platform
The physical phone is disposable and will be replaced yearly; the platform is what has staying power
money_quotes: Today's message is pretty simple: Apple is going for iPhone market share in a big, big way.
Instead of building a better $400 iPhone, they worked on halving the price of last year's phone.
At $199, and with the expansion to 70 countries around the world, Apple is going to sell a shitload of iPhones.
There's no longer any question whether Apple is going to sell 10 million iPhones in 2008. 20 million sounds more like it to me.
The physical phone is not the story.
The platform is the story. Platforms have staying power, and, once entrenched, are very hard to displace.
key_insight: Apple's iPhone 3G strategy was never about better hardware — it was about using an aggressive price cut to grab market share and then locking users into the platform through the App Store.
